title: How to Check Temperature of Grilled Meat Loaf on a BBQ
meta_description: Stop guessing if your BBQ meatloaf is done. Learn the ideal grilled meatloaf internal temperature and how to use an instant-read thermometer for juicy results.
html:
There is something uniquely satisfying about a meatloaf cooked over an open flame. You get that smoky char on the outside while keeping the center tender. However, the biggest challenge with grilling a loaf is the thickness. If you pull it off too early, you have raw centers; wait too long, and you're eating a dense, dry brick.

The only way to ensure consistency is by monitoring the grilled meatloaf internal temperature. Relying on the "finger test" or cutting into the loaf to check the color lets the juices escape, which defeats the purpose of slow-grilling. To get it right every time, you need a reliable tool like the Instant-Read Meat Thermometer to get a precise reading in seconds.

The Magic Number: What is the Ideal Grilled Meatloaf Internal Temperature?

Depending on the type of meat you are using, your target temperature will vary. For a traditional beef or pork-based meatloaf, the USDA recommends an internal temperature of 160°F (71°C). This ensures that any harmful bacteria are eliminated while keeping the meat moist.

If you are making a turkey or chicken meatloaf, you need to aim slightly higher, targeting 165°F. Because poultry is leaner than beef, it can dry out quickly, so it is critical to pull the meat off the grill the moment it hits that mark.

Keep in mind the concept of "carryover cooking." When you remove meatloaf from the grill, the internal temperature will continue to rise by about 5 degrees while it rests. For a beef loaf, pulling it at 155°F often results in a perfect 160°F after a ten-minute rest.

Where Exactly to Insert Your Thermometer

Placement is everything. If your probe is touching a piece of chunky onion, a slice of mushroom, or the metal foil of a grilling tray, you will get a false reading. To find the true grilled meatloaf internal temperature, you have to target the thickest part of the loaf.

Insert the probe horizontally into the center of the side of the loaf. Ensure the tip of the sensor is dead-center. If you are using a large, thick loaf, I recommend checking three different spots: the center, and slightly toward each end. This ensures there are no "cold spots" where the meat remains undercooked.

This is where an Instant-Read Meat Thermometer is a lifesaver. Instead of leaving a probe in for an hour, you can quickly poke, check, and pull, meaning you aren't letting all the heat out of your grill while you wait for a slow analog dial to move.

Best Grilling Methods for Even Heating

To avoid a burnt exterior and raw interior, avoid cooking your meatloaf directly over high heat. Use the two-zone grilling method:

  • Direct Heat Zone: Use this for 5-10 minutes at the start to sear the outside of the loaf.
  • Indirect Heat Zone: Move the loaf to the cooler side of the grill and close the lid. This turns your BBQ into an oven, allowing the heat to penetrate the center slowly.
  • The Foil Boat: Place your loaf on a piece of heavy-duty foil or a cast-iron skillet to prevent the bottom from charring too quickly.

By using indirect heat, you give yourself a wider window of time to monitor the temperature without worrying about the glaze burning.

Avoiding Common Temperature Mistakes

One of the most common mistakes is checking the temperature too often. Every time you open the grill lid, you lose a significant amount of heat, which extends the cooking time and can dry out the meat. Only start checking the internal temp when you are within 15-20 minutes of your expected finish time.

Another error is forgetting to calibrate or use a high-quality sensor. Cheap thermometers can be off by 10 degrees, which is the difference between a juicy dinner and an overcooked one. Using a professional-grade Instant-Read Meat Thermometer removes the guesswork and the anxiety of serving undercooked meat to your guests.

The Importance of the Resting Period

Once your thermometer hits the target temperature, move the meatloaf to a cutting board or platter. Resist the urge to slice into it immediately. Let the meat rest for 10 to 15 minutes.

During this time, the muscle fibers relax and reabsorb the juices. If you cut it immediately, those juices run out onto the board, leaving the meat dry. Resting also allows that carryover cooking to finish the job, bringing the grilled meatloaf internal temperature to the final safe zone.

FAQ

Can I use a meatloaf that was previously frozen?

Yes, but it is highly recommended to thaw it in the refrigerator overnight first. Cooking a frozen loaf on the BBQ often leads to a burnt exterior and a frozen center. If you must cook from frozen, use a lower temperature and a longer indirect heat cycle.

Why is the outside of my meatloaf burnt but the inside still raw?

This happens when the grill temperature is too high or you are cooking directly over the flames. Switch to indirect heat and use a lid to create a convection effect, which cooks the center more evenly.

How long should I leave the thermometer in the meat?

If you are using an instant-read style, you only need to keep the probe in for a few seconds until the digital reading stabilizes. This minimizes heat loss and keeps the meatloaf intact.
